name: ‘Abdu’lláh Páshá
summary: An Ottoman government official of the Baghdád period who, with his lieutenant Maḥmúd Áqá, was gradually drawn into contact with Bahá’u’lláh and helped spread word of His growing fame.
importance_reason: Single GPB mention; minor Baghdád-era official noted only for noising abroad Bahá’u’lláh's fame (not the ‘Akká barracks namesake).

name: ‘Abdu’lláh Ḵhán-i-Turkamán
summary: A military commander whom the Sháh, by farmán, ordered to recruit forces against the Bábís; he and over four hundred of his men perished in the fighting.
importance_reason: Recurring DB presence (7 mentions) as a named royal commander in the anti-Bábí campaign who, with many of his men, was killed.

name: ‘Abdu’lláh, brother of Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán
aliases: ‘Abdu’lláh Ḵhán, brother of Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Ḵhán
summary: Brother of Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán, a named participant in the events recounted in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Incidental DB figure (3 mentions) identified only by family relation to Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán.

name: ‘Abdu’lláh, son of Ustád Qurbán-‘Alíy-i-Banná
summary: A Bábí, son of Ustád Qurbán-‘Alíy-i-Banná, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental name identified only as the son of Ustád Qurbán-‘Alíy-i-Banná.

name: ‘Abdu’lláh-i-Qazvíní
summary: A maker of shrouds in whose service Siyyid Muḥammad (the Antichrist of the Bahá’í Revelation) became a vendor of goods.
importance_reason: One-mention neutral GPB figure, notable only as the shroud-maker employer of Siyyid Muḥammad-i-Iṣfahání.

name: ‘Abdu’r-Rasúl-i-Qumí
summary: A Bahá’í of the ‘Akká period who was savagely disemboweled at dawn while carrying water from the river to the Most Great House.
importance_reason: One-mention GPB martyr with a memorable, specific death scene serving the household of Bahá’u’lláh.

name: ‘Abdu’r-Raḥmán Pás̱há, Mutaṣarrif of ‘Akká
summary: Mutaṣarrif (governor) of ‘Akká who, with Muḥammad-Yúsuf Páshá, resolved to seize Bahá’u’lláh's followers but was telegraphically dismissed the very next morning and never again given a post.
importance_reason: Named ‘Akká-period antagonist (2 GPB mentions) whose plotting and abrupt providential dismissal is a taught episode.

name: ‘Abdu’ṣ-Ṣáliḥ, the gardener of the Riḍván at ‘Akká
aliases: ‘Abdu’ṣ-Ṣáliḥ, the gardener of the Riḍván at ‘Akká
summary: The gardener of the Riḍván garden at ‘Akká, named in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental DB figure remembered as the gardener of the Riḍván at ‘Akká.

name: ‘Alí Big, captain of the Nayrízí soldiers
common_name: ‘Alí Big, captain of the Nayrízí soldiers
summary: Captain of the Nayrízí soldiers who killed Mas̱hhadí Báqir and severed his head, giving it to a child.
importance_reason: One-mention DB combatant remembered only for a single brutal killing at Nayríz.

name: ‘Alí Khán of Savád-Kúh
summary: A man summoned by ‘Abbás-Qulí Ḵhán-i-Láríjání alongside Muḥammad-Sulṭán, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental name with no described role beyond being summoned.

name: ‘Alí Khán-i-Máh-Kú’í
aliases: the warden of the fortress of Máh-Kú
summary: The domineering warden of the fortress of Máh-Kú during the Báb's imprisonment (1847–1848); initially harsh, he was so transformed by a vision of the Báb praying by the river that he relaxed the conditions of confinement.
importance_reason: Heavily featured (21 mentions, DB+GPB); his conversion-by-vision and relaxing of the Báb's imprisonment is a famous, widely-taught Máh-Kú episode.
characterizations: Wikipedia/Bahá'í histories: the warden of the castle of Máh-Kú was a rough, rude and extremely strict man at the start of the Báb's incarceration
Bahá'í accounts: after a vision of the Báb standing by the river praying, ‘Alí Khán was so captivated that he lifted the strict constraints of the Báb's confinement

name: ‘Alí Ḵhán-i-Sangsarí
summary: A Bábí martyr from Sang-Sar, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention martyr-list entry from Sang-Sar with no further detail.

name: ‘Alí-Akbar Big
summary: Son of Muḥammad Big and a fervent believer in the Cause of Bahá’u’lláh, known as such among the Bahá’ís of Persia; met by Mírzá Abu’l-Faḍl.
importance_reason: One-mention DB believer noted only as a devoted Bahá’í and son of Muḥammad Big.

name: ‘Alí-Akbar, captain of Ḵhuy
summary: A military captain of Ḵhuy who arrived with infantry, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental officer with no role beyond arriving with troops.

name: ‘Alí-Akbar, son of Ustád Qurbán-‘Alíy-i-Banná
summary: A Bábí, son of Ustád Qurbán-‘Alíy-i-Banná, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental name identified only as the son of Ustád Qurbán-‘Alíy-i-Banná.

name: ‘Alí-Aḥmad-i-Sangsarí
summary: A Bábí martyr from Sang-Sar, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention martyr-list entry from Sang-Sar with no further detail.

name: ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har
summary: A twenty-seven-year-old Bahá’í youth strangled at Yazd in 1903, his body handed to a mob and dragged through the streets.
importance_reason: One-mention GPB martyr of the 1903 Yazd upheaval with a specific, memorable death.

name: ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har (Yazd, brother)
summary: One of two brothers in their early twenties beheaded with Muḥammad-Ḥasan in the Maydán-i-Ḵhán during the 1903 Yazd persecutions.
importance_reason: One-mention GPB martyr; a distinct Yazd brother-pair victim (disambiguated from the strangled ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har).

name: ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har Khán (elder brother of Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín Khán)
summary: Elder brother of Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín Khán, despatched with a thousand men against the Bábís; he perished and two of his sons were captured.
importance_reason: Recurring DB antagonist (4 mentions) who led a thousand-man force and died, with named descendants in the narrative.

name: ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har Ḵhán-i-Du-Dungi’í
summary: A figure named once in the Dawn-Breakers, identified by the locale Du-Dunqih.
importance_reason: Bare one-mention name with no described role.

name: ‘Alí-Mardán Khán
summary: A figure whose name attaches to a formidable citadel, the 'House of ‘Alí-Mardán Khán,' mentioned in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Two DB mentions but only as the eponym of a citadel, not as an actor in events.

name: ‘Alí-Qulí-i-Mázindarání
summary: A Bábí martyr of Mázindarán, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention martyr-list entry from Mázindarán with no further detail.

name: ‘Alí-S̱hawkat Páshá
summary: A non-Bahá’í dignitary at whose request Bahá’u’lláh composed a celebrated commentary on a Muḥammadan tradition, which excited his unbounded admiration.
importance_reason: One GPB mention, but notable as the recipient who occasioned a renowned commentary by Bahá’u’lláh.

name: ‘Azíz (uncle of Bahá'u'lláh)
summary: An uncle of Bahá’u’lláh who alone dared to oppose and dispute His views, prompting him to seek the aid of the mujtahid of Núr, Mullá Muḥammad.
importance_reason: One-mention DB figure, but with a distinct anecdote as the kinsman who challenged the young Bahá’u’lláh.

name: ‘Azíz Páshá
summary: An Ottoman official who formed a profound attachment to ‘Abdu’l-Bahá in Adrianople and, after promotion to Válí, twice visited ‘Akká to pay his respects to Bahá’u’lláh.
importance_reason: Three GPB mentions; a sympathetic Ottoman official whose devotion to ‘Abdu’l-Bahá and pilgrimage-like visits are a recurring detail.

name: ‘Aẓím-i-Mázindarání
summary: A Bábí martyr of Mázindarán, named once in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention martyr-list entry from Mázindarán with no further detail.

name: ‘Izzat Áqá
summary: The owner of the house in Adrianople to which Bahá’u’lláh moved and where He lived until His departure, during which period a number of Tablets were revealed.
importance_reason: Two GPB mentions, notable only as the owner of a Bahá’u’lláh residence in Adrianople.

name: ‘Umar Effendi
summary: A Turkish major appointed to accompany the exiles to ‘Akká who, at Bahá’u’lláh's insistence, became the instrument by which a harsh order against the exiles was revoked at Gallipoli (1868).
importance_reason: Two GPB mentions; a minor Ottoman officer whose intercession altered an order affecting the exiles.

name: ‘Umar ibn al-Fárid
summary: The great Egyptian Sufi poet (1181–1234), 'Sultan of the Lovers,' whose celebrated Tá’íyyih ode was the model for a poem; GPB notes Bahá’u’lláh's verses in the same rhyme and meter were acclaimed as surpassing it.
importance_reason: Externally a major Sufi poet, and in-corpus the literary benchmark Bahá’u’lláh's poetry was judged to surpass — a taught point of literary station.
characterizations: Wikipedia: ‘Umar ibn al-Fárid (b. 22 March 1181, d. 1234/35, Cairo) is regarded as the greatest and most influential Arabic Sufi poet, known as 'Sultan of the Lovers' (Sulṭān al-‘Áshiqīn)
Wikipedia: his masterpiece al-Tá’iyya al-Kubrá ('the Greater Poem Rhyming in T'), some 760 verses long, inspired numerous commentaries in the school of Ibn al-‘Arabī

name: ‘Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭáb
summary: The second Caliph of Islam (c. 583–644 CE); GPB invokes him as the archetype of usurped successorship, paralleling Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí's attempt to seize Bahá’u’lláh's appointed succession.
importance_reason: A world-historical Islamic figure, cited in GPB as the analogy for Covenant-breaking usurpation of succession — useful background for the Covenant narrative.
characterizations: Widely documented: ‘Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭáb (c. 583–644 CE), titled al-Fárúq, was the second of the Rashidun Caliphs and a senior companion of the Prophet Muḥammad

name: ‘Ábid
summary: A Bábí named among the companions, father of Náṣir (later named Ḥájí ‘Abbás), in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental Bábí listed among a group, with a son later renamed.

name: ‘Árif Bey
summary: Head of the four-officer Commission of Inquiry dispatched with plenary powers to ‘Akká by the Sulṭán against ‘Abdu’l-Bahá; he was shot dead by a sentry while fleeing the Young Turks (d. 1908).
importance_reason: Three GPB mentions; leader of the hostile ‘Akká Commission whose dramatic providential death is a recounted episode of the Covenant period.

name: ‘Údí Khammár
summary: Owner of the house in ‘Akká (c. 1873) to which Bahá’u’lláh was transferred and where the Kitáb-i-Aqdas-period Tablets were revealed; his house adjoined that of ‘Abbúd.
importance_reason: One GPB mention but a well-known place-association: the house of ‘Údí Khammár is a recognized site of Bahá’u’lláh's residence in ‘Akká.
